title.en-CA: When provincial and federal laws cover the same ground

summary.en-CA: Canada divides law-making between Parliament and the provincial legislatures, each with its own areas of authority set out in the Constitution. The boundaries are not always sharp, so a province and Parliament can both have laws touching the same subject. When a valid federal law and a valid provincial law genuinely conflict, the federal law prevails to the extent of the conflict, a rule called federal paramountcy. The provincial law is not erased; it stops operating only where it actually conflicts. This means a province can pass a law on something already addressed in federal law. Whether two such laws duplicate, complement, or conflict is a specific legal question about those particular laws, not something that follows from the overlap alone.

key_points.en-CA: Canada's Constitution assigns different areas of law-making to Parliament (federal) and to the provincial legislatures. Some areas belong to one level only; in a few, both can legislate.
Because the boundaries between these areas are not always sharp, both levels can end up with laws that touch the same subject.

When a valid federal and a valid provincial law genuinely conflict, the federal law prevails to the extent of the conflict. This is called federal paramountcy. The provincial law is not struck down; it simply stops operating where it conflicts.
Because of this, a province can enact a law on something already addressed federally.

Whether two laws on the same subject duplicate, complement, or conflict with one another is a specific legal question about those particular statutes. It does not follow from the fact of overlap alone.
